title: Midway: Four Minutes That Turned the Pacific War
subtitle: June 4, 1942 — The Battle That Broke Japan's Naval Supremacy
tagline: Japan had the largest carrier fleet in the world. America had broken their code. Four minutes of dive bombing changed everything.
viral_hook: Japan's carriers were refueling and rearming planes on deck. Four American dive bombers arrived in 4 minutes. Three carriers sank by sunset.
youtube_title: Battle of Midway 1942: The 4 Minutes That Won the Pacific War
lesson: Intelligence advantage used at exactly the right moment is worth more than any weapon.

scenes.title: The Code That Changed Everything
scenes.narration: May 1942. In a basement in Pearl Harbor, a team of American code-breakers is reading Japanese naval communications. They have cracked JN-25, the Imperial Japanese Navy's operational cipher. They know that Japan is planning a massive operation against a target they call 'AF.' Commander Joseph Rochefort suspects AF is Midway Atoll — the tiny island chain fifteen hundred miles northwest of Hawaii. To confirm it, he has Midway send a false radio message complaining about a broken water distillation plant. Two days later, a Japanese intercept reports that AF has a water shortage. The trap is set. Japan is sailing into an ambush they don't know exists.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The basement code-breaking operation at Pearl Harbor — analysts bent over Japanese intercepts, the cipher machines, the moment of recognition. Commander Rochefort's face as the confirmation comes. The strategic deception: Midway's false water message. Intelligence as the decisive weapon. 16:9.

scenes.title: Japan After Pearl Harbor
scenes.narration: Six months after Pearl Harbor, Japan controlled the largest empire in Pacific history. It held the Philippines, Malaya, Singapore, Burma, the Dutch East Indies, and a vast arc of Pacific islands from the Aleutians to the Solomon Islands. Admiral Isoroku Yamamoto, who had planned Pearl Harbor, knew the victory was incomplete: America's aircraft carriers had not been in port on December 7. They were still at sea, still a threat. His plan for Midway was designed to lure the American carriers into a decisive battle and destroy them — eliminating American naval power in the Pacific for years. He assembled the largest naval force Japan had ever sent to sea.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. A map of Japanese Pacific conquests by June 1942 — an empire stretching across the ocean. Admiral Yamamoto planning the Midway operation: the massive fleet assembled, the carriers, the battleships, the transports. Japan at the height of its power, about to overreach. 16:9.

scenes.title: Yamamoto — The Admiral Who Feared Victory
scenes.narration: Admiral Isoroku Yamamoto was Japan's greatest naval strategist and one of its most reluctant warriors. He had studied at Harvard, served as naval attaché in Washington, and knew American industrial power better than almost any Japanese officer alive. He had opposed the war, telling Prince Konoe that he could run wild for six months to a year, but had no confidence in the second and third years. Pearl Harbor was his plan, executed against his strategic judgment. At Midway, he assembled an overwhelming force: four fleet carriers, hundreds of aircraft, and a supporting armada so large that the operation plan required twelve separate task forces. He brought too much. He also brought the wrong assumption: that America didn't know he was coming.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Admiral Yamamoto — intelligent, composed, carrying the burden of a war he didn't want. His fleet at sea: the massive carrier Akagi, the Kaga, Soryu, Hiryu — four of the finest carriers in the world. The overwhelming force assembled. The admiral who understood what he was risking. 16:9.

scenes.title: Nimitz — The Admiral Who Waited
scenes.narration: Admiral Chester Nimitz had commanded the Pacific Fleet since December 31, 1941 — appointed days after Pearl Harbor. He was calm, methodical, and completely trusted his intelligence. When Rochefort's team told him Japan was targeting Midway in early June, Nimitz made the decision to commit his entire carrier force — Enterprise, Hornet, and the hastily repaired Yorktown — to an ambush. He was betting everything on broken code and a deception. He gave his carrier commanders one instruction: inflict maximum damage. He could not replace his carriers. He was sending them to a battle where Japan outnumbered him massively, relying on surprise and the accuracy of his intelligence. He was right about the date, the place, and the plan. He was right about everything.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Admiral Nimitz — steady, confident, a man who trusts his intelligence and his commanders. The three American carriers: Enterprise, Hornet, Yorktown (still dripping from emergency repairs). The American force: smaller, outgunned, but knowing exactly what's coming. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Yorktown's Miracle Repair
scenes.narration: USS Yorktown had been badly damaged at the Battle of Coral Sea just weeks before Midway. Navy engineers estimated three months of repairs. Nimitz gave them seventy-two hours. Working around the clock, with welders and workers swarming the ship in Pearl Harbor, the Yorktown was patched well enough to fight in three days. It was not fully repaired. Compartments were still flooded. Temporary fixes held what should have been permanent ones. But when the Yorktown sailed for Midway on May 30, it was operational. That decision — to push the Yorktown to sea — gave Nimitz three carriers instead of two. It gave him, as it turned out, exactly the margin he needed.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Yorktown in dry dock at Pearl Harbor — hundreds of workers swarming the damaged carrier. Welders, engineers, workers in shifts around the clock. The ship emerging from repairs in 72 hours. The hastily repaired Yorktown sailing, still showing damage, into the most important battle of the Pacific War. 16:9.

scenes.title: June 4 — The Morning Attack
scenes.narration: June 4, 1942. At dawn, Japan launched its first wave — 108 aircraft — against Midway Island. The attack hit Midway hard but failed to neutralize it. The Japanese strike commander radioed back: a second attack on Midway is necessary. This message set off the sequence of decisions that would destroy four Japanese carriers. Admiral Nagumo, commanding the carrier force, had a second wave of aircraft armed with torpedoes for anti-ship attack — reserved for the American carriers. Now he had to decide: re-arm them with bombs for a second Midway strike, or keep them ready for ships. He ordered the re-arming. His planes' torpedoes were unloaded and bombs loaded. Then, thirty minutes later, an American carrier was reported.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Japanese aircraft launching from carriers at dawn — Zero fighters, Vals, Kates taking off from the Akagi and Kaga. The attack on Midway. Then the critical moment on the carrier deck: torpedoes being unloaded, bombs being loaded. The decision being made that would cost Japan everything. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Failed American Attacks
scenes.narration: American attacks through the morning were disastrous. Midway-based aircraft attacked repeatedly and were slaughtered. Torpedo Squadron 8 from the Hornet — fifteen aircraft — found the Japanese carriers and attacked without fighter escort. Every plane was shot down. Every crew member was killed, except one: Ensign George Gay, who floated in the water and watched the battle unfold from the sea. Torpedo Squadron 6 and Torpedo Squadron 3 suffered similar losses. Forty-one torpedo bombers attacked; thirty-five were shot down. Not one torpedo hit a Japanese carrier. The Japanese combat air patrol — Zero fighters — was down at low altitude, destroying the torpedo planes, when the dive bombers arrived.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. American torpedo bombers — TBD Devastators — attacking Japanese carriers through storms of anti-aircraft fire and Zero fighters. Planes going down in flames. The sacrifice of Torpedo Squadron 8: fifteen planes, fourteen shot down. Ensign George Gay floating alone in the Pacific watching. The devastating toll. 16:9.

scenes.title: Four Minutes
scenes.narration: 10:22 AM. American dive bombers from Enterprise and Yorktown arrive over the Japanese fleet almost simultaneously — by accident, not coordination. Lieutenant Commander Wade McClusky of Enterprise had searched for an hour, nearly out of fuel, before spotting a Japanese destroyer's wake and following it to the fleet. Lieutenant Commander Max Leslie of Yorktown arrived from a different direction. Below them: three Japanese carriers — Akagi, Kaga, Soryu — with their decks crowded with fully fueled and armed aircraft being prepared for launch. The Zeros were all at low altitude from the torpedo attack. The sky above the carriers was clear. What followed took approximately four minutes.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. American SBD Dauntless dive bombers tipping over into their attack dives — the view from the cockpit looking down at the Japanese carriers below. The carrier decks visible: planes, fuel lines, bombs. The Zeros at low altitude, unable to climb in time. The four minutes beginning. 16:9.

scenes.title: Three Carriers Burning
scenes.narration: In four minutes, three Japanese fleet carriers were fatally hit. Akagi — Yamamoto's former flagship — took one bomb through the flight deck that detonated among the armed and fueled aircraft below. Kaga took four bombs. Soryu took three. All three ships were immediately engulfed in catastrophic secondary explosions as the fueled, armed aircraft on their decks detonated. By afternoon, all three were abandoned and sinking. The fourth Japanese carrier, Hiryu, launched a counterattack that hit Yorktown twice, eventually sinking her. Then dive bombers from Enterprise found Hiryu and sent four bombs through her deck. By evening, all four Japanese fleet carriers at Midway were sinking or sunk.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Three Japanese carriers burning simultaneously — the Akagi, Kaga, and Soryu engulfed in explosions and fire. The secondary detonations of the armed aircraft. Then the Hiryu's counterattack hitting Yorktown. Then Hiryu herself hit and burning. Four of Japan's finest carriers, all destroyed in one day. 16:9.

scenes.title: What Japan Lost
scenes.narration: At Midway, Japan lost four fleet carriers — Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, Hiryu — along with over two hundred aircraft and, most critically, over three thousand experienced pilots, aircrew, and carrier aviation specialists. These were the veterans of Pearl Harbor, Coral Sea, and every Japanese carrier operation since the war began. Japan had the industrial capacity to build new carriers. It could not quickly replace the trained carrier aviators. The Hiryu's aircrews, who had been training for years, were irreplaceable. Japan's carrier aviation never fully recovered. The strategic momentum that had driven Japan's Pacific expansion for six months stopped at Midway and never came back.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Japanese carriers sinking — the Akagi going down, the sea around it dotted with survivors. The scale of the loss: four carriers, over two hundred aircraft, three thousand men. Japan's carrier aviation veterans going down with their ships. The irreplaceable human cost. 16:9.

scenes.title: Yorktown's End
scenes.narration: The USS Yorktown, hit twice by Hiryu's aircraft, was abandoned June 4. On June 6, while under tow, a Japanese submarine found her and fired two torpedoes. She sank on June 7. The ship that had been repaired in seventy-two hours fought at Midway, absorbed two air attacks, and survived long enough to almost make it home. Her crew mourned her. Admiral Fletcher, who had commanded from her bridge, was rescued and later gave command of the surviving carriers to Admiral Spruance. The Yorktown was discovered on the seabed in 1998 by Robert Ballard — the same man who found the Titanic — remarkably intact, sitting upright on the ocean floor two miles down.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Yorktown listing and abandoned at sea — her crew going over the side. Then the submarine's attack: the torpedoes striking. The carrier sinking finally on June 7. Then the discovery in 1998: Robert Ballard's team finding her remarkably intact on the ocean floor, two miles down. 16:9.

scenes.title: Ensign Gay's View
scenes.narration: Ensign George Gay was the only survivor of Torpedo Squadron 8's fifteen aircraft. Shot down during the attack, he floated in the Pacific Ocean for hours, hiding under a seat cushion when Japanese ships came close, watching the battle unfold around him. He saw the Japanese carriers burning. He saw the Hiryu's strike group launching. He saw the battle from the most extraordinary vantage point in the Pacific War — a man in the water, alone, watching history happen around him. He was rescued the next day. He lived until 1994. He spent fifty years describing what he saw from the sea, and no account of Midway is complete without his testimony.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Ensign George Gay floating in the Pacific — a lone figure in a life jacket, hiding under a seat cushion as Japanese ships pass nearby. Around him: the Japanese carriers burning in the distance. The entire battle visible to one man in the water. The most extraordinary witness to the most important battle of the Pacific War. 16:9.

scenes.title: Why Japan Lost
scenes.narration: Japan lost at Midway for multiple reasons that compounded each other. First: American code-breaking eliminated the element of surprise. Second: Admiral Nagumo's decision to re-arm his aircraft with bombs for a second Midway strike, then reverse it when carriers were reported, left his decks crowded with armed planes and fuel lines at the worst possible moment. Third: the sacrifice of the torpedo squadrons, though devastating, pulled the Japanese combat air patrol to low altitude. Fourth: the dive bombers arrived at exactly the moment the carriers were most vulnerable. None of these factors alone would have been decisive. Together, they produced four burning carriers in one afternoon.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The chain of Japanese decisions visualized: Nagumo's re-arming order, the armed planes on deck, the Zeros at low altitude. Each decision connecting to the next. The convergence of American code-breaking, torpedo squadron sacrifice, and dive bomber timing. Four factors making one catastrophe. 16:9.

scenes.title: Yamamoto's Reaction
scenes.narration: Yamamoto received the news of the carrier losses on his flagship Yamato — the largest battleship ever built — which was far behind the carrier force. He initially considered pressing the attack with his surface ships. His staff talked him out of it. Without air cover, his battleships were vulnerable to American air attack. He ordered the operation cancelled and withdrew. Yamamoto never commanded a fleet in battle again after Midway. In April 1943, American code-breakers again read Japanese communications: Yamamoto's itinerary for an inspection tour was transmitted in cipher that the Americans broke. P-38 fighters were waiting for his transport. He was shot down and killed over Bougainville. Code-breaking killed him twice.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Yamamoto on the bridge of the Yamato receiving the catastrophic news. His face: the architect of Pearl Harbor understanding what has happened. The withdrawal order given. Then his final flight in 1943: the transport plane over Bougainville, the P-38s waiting, the shoot-down. Code-breaking ending his career and his life. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Pacific War Turns
scenes.narration: After Midway, Japan never again launched a major offensive naval operation in the Pacific. The strategic initiative passed to the United States. Guadalcanal began two months later — America attacking, Japan defending. The island-hopping campaign that would eventually reach Japan's home islands was made possible by Midway. Japan still had a powerful navy, still had experienced soldiers, still had the will to fight. What it no longer had was the offensive carrier capability to threaten American control of the central Pacific. Midway didn't end the Pacific War — that took three more years and cost hundreds of thousands of lives. But it determined the direction the war would go.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Pacific War's turning point visualized: a map showing the shift from Japanese expansion to American advance after Midway. Guadalcanal, the Solomon Islands campaign, the march across the Pacific. The strategic momentum that Midway transferred and never gave back. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Code-Breakers
scenes.narration: The Battle of Midway was won in a basement in Pearl Harbor before a single plane took off. Commander Joseph Rochefort and his team at Station HYPO broke enough of JN-25 to give Nimitz the date, location, and order of battle for the Japanese operation. Their work was so sensitive that it was classified for decades after the war. Rochefort himself was denied official recognition for years — the result of bureaucratic rivalry with naval intelligence in Washington. He was finally awarded the Distinguished Service Medal posthumously in 1985 — forty-three years after the battle he won. The most important weapon at Midway was never photographed or put on display. It was a man reading enemy mail in a basement.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Commander Rochefort and his code-breaking team in their basement at Pearl Harbor — the pipes overhead, the cipher machines, the decoded messages. The intelligence product that won Midway. Then the irony: Rochefort denied recognition for decades. His posthumous medal in 1985. The invisible heroes of the decisive battle. 16:9.

scenes.title: Miracle at Midway
scenes.narration: Midway has been called a miracle — the underdog American Navy defeating the overwhelmingly superior Japanese force through luck and courage. The luck was real: the timing of the dive bomber arrival, the Zeros at low altitude, the crowded carrier decks. But the preparation was not luck. Nimitz's decision to trust his intelligence was a calculated risk, not a miracle. Rochefort's code-breaking was skill, not fortune. The torpedo squadrons' sacrifice, though devastating, was purposeful. Fletcher and Spruance positioned their carriers correctly. The pilots trained for years executed their attacks under fire. What looks like a miracle was competent people doing their jobs at the worst possible moment with everything on the line.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The multiple elements of the Midway 'miracle' deconstructed: intelligence, positioning, sacrifice, execution. Each one the product of human skill and decision. The miracle as the sum of many competent acts under impossible pressure. Not luck — preparation meeting opportunity. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Wreck of the Yorktown
scenes.narration: In 1998, Robert Ballard — who found the Titanic in 1985 — located the Yorktown on the floor of the Pacific, seventeen thousand feet down, remarkably intact. She sits upright, her flight deck still recognizable, artifacts of her crew visible in the silt. Anti-aircraft guns still point at a sky they can no longer reach. A boot. A telephone. The everyday objects of a warship that fought, survived hits, and finally slipped under the Pacific on June 7, 1942. The discovery reminded the world that Midway was not just strategy and code-breaking. It was also men who went to sea and did not come back.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Yorktown on the ocean floor — the deep-sea footage from Ballard's expedition. The remarkably intact carrier 17,000 feet down. Anti-aircraft guns still pointing upward. Artifacts in the silt. The human dimension of the battle preserved in the deep. 16:9.

scenes.title: Intelligence as a Weapon
scenes.narration: Midway is the clearest example in military history of intelligence superiority overcoming material inferiority. The United States had three carriers; Japan had four at Midway plus many more in reserve. Japan had more aircraft, more ships, more experienced aviators. In every material measure, Japan should have won. The one asymmetry was information: America knew where Japan was going, when, and with what. That asymmetry made all the others irrelevant. Midway is taught in every intelligence and military school in the world as the proof that knowing what your enemy is doing can be worth more than any weapon system. The lesson is still being applied, in different forms, in every conflict today.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The intelligence advantage visualized: the Americans knowing the Japanese order of battle, timing, and target while Japan remains ignorant of the ambush. The material balance — Japan stronger — overridden by the information balance. The lesson applied to modern intelligence operations. 16:9.

scenes.title: Japan's Strategic Mistake
scenes.narration: Midway exposed a fundamental flaw in Japanese strategic thinking in 1942: overextension. The Midway operation was designed to force a decisive battle, but Japan was simultaneously conducting operations in the Aleutian Islands, the Solomon Islands, and New Guinea. The forces were spread too thin. The Midway fleet was so large it was difficult to coordinate, and its very size contributed to Yamamoto's confidence that nothing could go wrong. Japan had won so consistently for six months that its commanders began to assume they could not lose. That assumption — that the enemy would behave as expected, that surprise was guaranteed — was the actual casualty of Midway.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Japan's overextension mapped: simultaneous operations across the Pacific in mid-1942. The Midway fleet so large it creates logistical problems. The confidence of six months of victory becoming assumption. The moment when winning too much becomes a strategic liability. 16:9.

scenes.title: Midway Today
scenes.narration: Midway Atoll is today a National Wildlife Refuge — home to the world's largest colony of Laysan albatrosses, over a million birds. The runway from which American planes launched on June 4, 1942 is still there. The island is largely unchanged from the battle. Veterans' groups hold commemorations. The Battle of Midway is remembered every June 4. In 2019, a major Hollywood film retold the battle. The real story — Rochefort's code-breaking, McClusky's navigation, Gay's survival — is more extraordinary than any version Hollywood has produced. The atoll that changed the Pacific War is now a bird sanctuary. History moves on.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Midway Atoll today — the albatross colony, the peaceful lagoon, the old runways visible. The contrast: the world's most consequential naval battle fought here, now a wildlife refuge. Veterans at a commemoration. The birds unaware of what happened in the waters around them. 16:9.

scenes.title: Four Minutes in History
scenes.narration: The four minutes during which McClusky's and Leslie's dive bombers destroyed three Japanese carriers is among the most compressed instances of decisive action in military history. In those four minutes: the strategic balance of the Pacific War shifted. Japan's offensive carrier capability was broken. The initiative that had carried Japan from Pearl Harbor to the borders of Australia and India was extinguished. Four minutes of accurate bombing by approximately fifty dive bombers did what six months of American naval operations had not been able to accomplish. The pilots who put those bombs through the Japanese carrier decks probably did not understand in the moment what they had done. History took a few days to understand it too.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The four minutes compressed: the dive bombers rolling in, the bombs releasing, the impacts on three carrier decks simultaneously. The compression of consequence: four minutes determining the next three years of the Pacific War. The pilots not yet knowing what they've done. History catching up to the moment. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Day the Pacific Changed
scenes.narration: On June 4, 1942, the United States Navy lost one carrier and over three hundred aircraft. Japan lost four carriers, over three hundred aircraft, and thousands of irreplaceable carrier aviators. The material losses were roughly comparable. The strategic consequences were not. America could replace what it lost. Japan could not. Midway was not won by superior numbers or superior technology — it was won by superior intelligence, superior timing, and the willingness of torpedo squadron crews to fly into certain death so that dive bombers could arrive above undefended carrier decks four minutes later. The Pacific War ended three years after Midway. Its outcome was decided on June 4, 1942.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The final accounting: American losses vs Japanese losses. The replacement capacity that made American losses recoverable. The irreplaceable Japanese carrier aviators going down. The strategic consequence that dwarfed the material loss. The Pacific War's direction set on one June afternoon. 16:9.

scenes.title: Intelligence Won the Pacific
scenes.narration: Subscribe to Gods and Glory — the battles that actually changed history, told the way they actually happened. Next episode: The Battle of the Bulge — Hitler's last gamble, the frozen Ardennes, and the American soldiers who held the line when everything said they couldn't.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. A final image: the Pacific Ocean at dawn, the site of Midway — the albatrosses wheeling over the lagoon where carriers once burned. The peaceful water over the most consequential battle in Pacific history. Channel logo. Subscribe prompt. 16:9.

scenes.title: The Pacific War — January to May 1942
scenes.narration: In the six months after Pearl Harbor, Japan had seized an empire. The Philippines, Malaya, Singapore, Burma, the Dutch East Indies, Wake Island, Guam — all fell in the most rapid territorial expansion in modern military history. Japan's defensive perimeter now extended 4,000 miles from Tokyo. But Admiral Yamamoto Isoroku understood that Pearl Harbor had failed at its essential objective: the American aircraft carriers had been at sea and survived. Until the carriers were destroyed, the Pacific war was unfinished.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Map of Japanese expansion across the Pacific January-May 1942 — the vast new empire from Wake Island to Burma, the defensive perimeter extending 4,000 miles from Tokyo. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Joseph Rochefort — The Codebreaker in the Basement
scenes.narration: Commander Joseph Rochefort ran Station HYPO from a basement office in Pearl Harbor. He and his team of 30 cryptanalysts worked around the clock for weeks without going home, sleeping in the office, eating at their desks. They had partially broken JN-25, Japan's naval operational code. By May 1942 they were reading 15-20% of Japanese message traffic. That fraction was enough. When they identified the target as AF, Rochefort sent a message in the clear: Midway is low on fresh water. A Japanese signal promptly reported that AF was low on fresh water. The trap was set.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Commander Rochefort in the Station HYPO basement — the codebreaking team working by fluorescent lights surrounded by paper, the intelligence operation that located the Japanese fleet. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: AF — The Debate at Pearl Harbor
scenes.narration: Not everyone believed Rochefort. The intelligence section in Washington believed the Japanese target was the Aleutians or possibly the US West Coast. Rochefort was certain it was Midway. The fresh water test settled the argument. But Admiral Nimitz had to make a decision: send the carriers to Midway based on intelligence that could be wrong, leaving the West Coast undefended, or hold the carriers back. Nimitz trusted Rochefort. He ordered all three available carriers — Enterprise, Hornet, and the damaged Yorktown — to Midway.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Admiral Nimitz reviewing intelligence reports in his Pearl Harbor office, the decision to trust Rochefort's codebreaking and commit all three carriers to Midway — the command decision that changed the Pacific war. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Yorktown — 72 Hours
scenes.narration: USS Yorktown had been damaged at the Battle of Coral Sea. Naval engineers estimated repairs would take 90 days. Nimitz ordered repairs completed in three days. Working in round-the-clock shifts, 1,400 workers made the carrier combat-capable by June 2. She was not perfect — she left Pearl Harbor still leaking oil, with crews from other ships borrowed to fill out her air group. But she was operational. Japan's intelligence reported only two American carriers at Midway. There were three.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. 1,400 shipyard workers repairing the Yorktown in 72 hours — the welding lights burning around the clock, the damaged carrier transformed to combat-ready against all engineering logic. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Japanese Order of Battle — Overwhelming Force
scenes.narration: The Japanese fleet approaching Midway in late May 1942 was the most powerful naval force ever assembled. The Kido Butai — the carrier strike force — had four fleet carriers: Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, and Hiryu. Combined, they carried 250 aircraft. The invasion fleet behind them had 14 transports carrying 5,000 troops. A surface bombardment group had two battleships. The entire force totaled 200 ships. Against this, Nimitz had three carriers, no battleships, and the land-based aircraft on Midway island itself.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Japanese fleet — four carriers, battleships, cruisers, destroyers — the most powerful naval force ever assembled, moving northeast across the Pacific toward Midway. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Fatal Assumption — Japan's Intelligence Failure
scenes.narration: Japan's operational plan rested on a critical assumption: American carriers would not arrive at Midway until after the island was taken and the Japanese fleet had consolidated. This assumption was wrong because American codebreakers had read Yamamoto's orders. The entire strategic purpose of Operation MI — to draw out and destroy the American carriers in a decisive engagement — had already been negated before a single plane flew. The Japanese were trying to spring an ambush while their intended victims were already waiting in ambush position.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Japanese fleet intelligence officers confident in their operational timeline — the fatal gap between Japanese assumption and American foreknowledge, the ambush waiting to be sprung in reverse. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: June 3 — First Contact
scenes.narration: American search planes found elements of the Japanese invasion fleet on June 3 and land-based bombers from Midway attacked in waves. All attacks failed — the high-level bombing that existed in 1942 was almost useless against maneuvering warships. But the Japanese recognized they had lost surprise: American forces knew they were coming. Nagumo, commanding the carrier force, received the news that Midway was alert but continued on course. He had 250 carrier aircraft and the most battle-tested naval aviators in the world. He was not afraid of Midway.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Land-based bombers from Midway attacking the Japanese invasion fleet on June 3 — bombs falling wide of maneuvering warships, the first failed American attacks alerting Japan that surprise was lost. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: June 4 — The First Strike
scenes.narration: At 4:30 AM on June 4, Admiral Nagumo launched 108 aircraft at Midway island — bombers and fighters to suppress the airfield. The strike commander reported back: Midway needs a second strike to be neutralized for the invasion. Nagumo made the decision that would destroy his fleet. He ordered his reserve aircraft — armed with torpedoes for use against ships — to be re-armed with bombs for a second island strike. Mechanics began the laborious process of switching ordnance in the hangar decks. At that moment, American search planes found the Kido Butai.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Japanese aircraft launching from Kido Butai in the pre-dawn darkness of June 4, 1942 — 108 planes heading for Midway, the carrier decks cleared, the fatal re-arming order about to be given. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Torpedo Bombers — Sacrifice at Midway
scenes.narration: Three American torpedo bomber squadrons found the Japanese carriers and attacked without fighter cover. Torpedo Squadron 8, flying the obsolete Devastator, was destroyed almost entirely — 15 of 15 planes lost, 29 of 30 men killed. Torpedo Squadrons 6 and 3 suffered similar losses. Not a single torpedo hit. The attack seemed like complete failure. But those torpedo bombers had pulled the Japanese combat air patrol down to wave height to defend against them. The Zeros were at sea level, 15 miles away. And the dive bombers were at 20,000 feet, directly above the carriers.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. American torpedo bombers attacking Japanese carriers through intense anti-aircraft fire — Devastators falling into the sea, the suicidal attack pulling Japanese fighters to sea level, unknowingly clearing the sky above. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Five Minutes That Changed the War
scenes.narration: At 10:22 AM, dive bombers from Enterprise and Yorktown pushed over into their dives. The Japanese carriers had their decks crowded with re-armed aircraft, fueled and ready. Bombs hit Akagi and Kaga within seconds of each other. Soryu was hit moments later. The combination of aircraft fuel, loose ammunition, and aircraft crowded on the decks turned each carrier into an inferno. All three were burning and doomed within five minutes. The most powerful carrier force in the world had been destroyed in the time it takes to drink a cup of coffee.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. American dive bombers screaming down onto the Japanese carriers — Akagi, Kaga, and Soryu hit within minutes, the crowded flight decks exploding in aviation fuel and loose ordnance. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Hiryu Strikes Back
scenes.narration: The fourth carrier, Hiryu, had separated from the group and survived. Her commander, Yamaguchi Tamon, launched immediate counterstrikes. His aircraft found Yorktown and hit her with bombs and torpedoes. Two separate Hiryu strikes left Yorktown dead in the water, listing, abandoned. For several hours it appeared Japan might yet salvage the battle — one Japanese carrier versus two damaged American ones. Then dive bombers from Enterprise found Hiryu in the late afternoon and put four bombs into her flight deck. By evening, all four Japanese carriers were burning.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The surviving Hiryu launching counterstrikes at Yorktown, the American carrier hit and listing — and then Enterprise's dive bombers finding Hiryu, the last Japanese carrier going up in flames. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Carrier Crews — Burning with Their Ships
scenes.narration: The four Japanese carriers burned through the night of June 4-5. The crews of Akagi, Kaga, Soryu, and Hiryu fought their fires through the night before being ordered to abandon ship. Soryu sank in 20 minutes. Kaga sank at 7:25 PM. Akagi was scuttled by Japanese destroyers on June 5. Hiryu sank at 9 AM on June 5. Among the dead were some of Japan's most experienced naval aviators — men who had flown at Pearl Harbor, in the Indian Ocean campaign, at Coral Sea. Japan could build new carriers. She could not replace these men.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Japanese carriers burning through the night of June 4-5, crews fighting fires on doomed ships, the most experienced naval aviators in the world dying with their ships. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Yamamoto's Choice — Advance or Retreat
scenes.narration: Admiral Yamamoto, commanding from his flagship far to the west, received the news that all four carriers were lost. He still had the most powerful surface fleet afloat — battleships, cruisers, destroyers — against which American carriers were extremely vulnerable at night. He ordered an advance. Then he ordered a retreat. Then he ordered another advance. His staff officers could see he was in shock. By midnight he ordered the entire operation cancelled and the fleet withdrawn. The man who had planned Pearl Harbor understood what Midway meant. Japan had lost the war.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Admiral Yamamoto on his flagship receiving the news of four lost carriers — the architect of Pearl Harbor absorbing the catastrophe, his fleet advancing and retreating as he struggled to comprehend the defeat. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Yorktown's Survivors and Second Death
scenes.narration: Yorktown was damaged but not sunk on June 4. Repair crews went back aboard and she was under tow on June 6, still afloat. A Japanese submarine found her and fired two torpedoes. The Yorktown, which had survived Coral Sea, which had been repaired in 72 hours, which had fought at Midway under a crew stitched together from multiple ships, finally sank on June 7. 141 of her crew were killed in total. The destroyer Hammann alongside her when the torpedoes hit also sank, killing 80 more. The ship that gave America three carriers instead of two died hard.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Yorktown under tow after the battle, repair crews working aboard — and then the submarine torpedo explosion, the carrier that saved Midway finally sinking on June 7. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Admiral Spruance — The Invisible Victor
scenes.narration: Rear Admiral Raymond Spruance commanded the Enterprise and Hornet task force at Midway. He had replaced the ill Admiral Halsey two days before the battle. He had never commanded carriers before. His decision to launch aircraft at maximum range on the morning of June 4 — 175 miles from the Japanese carriers — was criticized as too risky. His decision not to pursue the Japanese fleet on the night of June 4-5 was criticized as too cautious. Both decisions were correct. Spruance won the greatest naval victory in American history on his first carrier command.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Admiral Spruance on Enterprise's bridge, the quiet methodical commander who replaced the flamboyant Halsey and won the greatest naval battle in American history on his first carrier command. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: What Midway Decided
scenes.narration: Japan lost four fleet carriers at Midway. America lost one. Japan lost 248 aircraft and their trained crews. America lost 150 aircraft but the crews were partly replaced. More importantly, Japan's strategic initiative — the ability to choose when and where to fight — was gone. From Midway forward, Japan would be reacting to American moves rather than making her own. The war in the Pacific would last another three years and cost millions of lives. But the question of who would win was answered on June 4, 1942.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Map showing the Pacific strategic balance shifting after Midway — Japan on defense, America beginning its island-hopping campaign, the three-year road to Tokyo beginning from this turning point. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Rochefort's Reward — Bureaucratic Revenge
scenes.narration: Commander Rochefort's codebreaking made Midway possible. The intelligence section in Washington, which had been wrong about the target, spent months after Midway trying to give credit to their own analysts. They succeeded. Rochefort was denied the Distinguished Service Medal he was recommended for and transferred to command a floating drydock — a dead-end assignment. He received the Presidential Medal of Freedom posthumously in 1986, 44 years after the battle. The man who cracked the code that won the Pacific war spent the rest of the war in institutional exile.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Commander Rochefort — the codebreaker who made Midway possible, denied recognition by institutional rivals, transferred to obscurity, his contribution acknowledged decades after his death. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Japanese Pilot Shortage — The Long Consequence
scenes.narration: The pilots lost at Midway could not be replaced quickly. Japan had an intensive training pipeline that prioritized quality over quantity — a fighter pilot required 700 hours of training before combat. The graduates of Japan's pre-war aviation program, who had combat experience from China, who had flown at Pearl Harbor, who had devastated British and American airpower across the Pacific — these men were irreplaceable. As Japan's remaining experienced pilots were ground down in subsequent battles, the quality gap between Japanese and American aviation widened until American pilots were routinely killing veterans with inexperienced pilots.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Japanese aviation training pipeline — the years required to develop expert pilots, the growing shortage after Midway, the quality gap that widened with each subsequent battle. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Island Campaign — What Midway Made Possible
scenes.narration: After Midway, America began the island-hopping campaign that would bring the war to Japan's home islands. Guadalcanal in August 1942. The Solomons. The Marshalls and Marianas in 1944. Iwo Jima and Okinawa in 1945. Each step brought American bombers closer to Tokyo. None of it was possible without Midway. The carriers Japan lost on June 4, 1942 could not protect Japan's island perimeter. The experience lost with those carriers could not train the replacement pilots fast enough. Midway didn't end the war — it decided who would win it.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Map of the American island-hopping campaign — Guadalcanal to Iwo Jima, the path to Tokyo made possible by the carrier superiority established at Midway. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Torpedo Squadron 8 — The Memorial
scenes.narration: Of Torpedo Squadron 8's 30 men, one survived: Ensign George Gay. Shot down and floating in the water, he watched the Japanese carriers burn from under his life raft. He was picked up the following day. Gay became the only firsthand witness to both the torpedo attack and the destruction of the Japanese fleet. The men of Torpedo Squadron 8, and of the other torpedo squadrons whose failed attack made the dive bomber strike possible, are remembered by a memorial on Midway island. They died in an attack that accomplished nothing directly and changed everything indirectly.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The memorial to Torpedo Squadron 8 on Midway island — the one survivor George Gay, the 29 men who died in a failed attack that unknowingly cleared the sky for the dive bombers above. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: June 4, 1942 — The Day the Pacific Changed
scenes.narration: In five minutes on June 4, 1942, the Pacific war was decided. Three Japanese carriers destroyed in a single dive bombing attack. The fourth carrier gone by evening. 250 aircraft and their irreplaceable crews at the bottom of the Pacific. The Japanese navy would build new carriers. It would never replace the Kido Butai. The six months of Japanese dominance that began at Pearl Harbor ended at Midway. The three years of American advance that ended in Tokyo Bay began there. Five minutes. Three carriers. The turning point of the largest naval war in history.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Pacific Ocean at Midway — three burning carriers visible from the air, the tactical map showing the battle's decisive moment, the strategic turning point of World War II in the Pacific. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Nagumo's Dilemma — The Re-Arming Decision
scenes.narration: The most consequential single decision at Midway was Admiral Nagumo's order to re-arm his reserve aircraft from torpedoes to bombs. The decision was reasonable given available information: his strike commander said Midway needed a second attack, and no confirmed report of American carriers had yet arrived. When a report of American ships arrived minutes later, Nagumo ordered another re-arming — back to torpedoes. His carriers were caught between configurations: planes sitting on the hangar deck with weapons being changed, fuel lines running, ammunition in the open. A weapons management problem became a catastrophic vulnerability.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Japanese carrier hangar decks during the fatal re-arming — aircraft with weapons being swapped, fuel hoses running between planes, ammunition in the open, the vulnerability that five minutes of dive bombing would exploit. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Search Pattern Gap
scenes.narration: Nagumo's search pattern for American ships on June 4 had a gap. A Japanese cruiser launched a search plane on a sector that would have found the American carriers — but the catapult malfunctioned. The plane launched 30 minutes late. By the time it reported American ships, Nagumo had already begun his re-arming. The report came too late to change the carrier configuration before American bombers arrived. The catapult malfunction on one cruiser, combined with the 30-minute delay, meant Japan received the critical American carrier sighting 90 minutes after they should have. Those 90 minutes were everything.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Japanese search aircraft launching late due to a catapult malfunction — the 30-minute delay that compressed Nagumo's decision timeline fatally, the mechanical failure that changed history. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Yamaguchi Tamon — The Admiral Who Stayed
scenes.narration: Rear Admiral Yamaguchi Tamon commanded the Hiryu and survived the June 4 sinkings by chance — his carrier was positioned away from the others. He launched the counterstrikes that damaged Yorktown. When Hiryu was hit late that afternoon, Yamaguchi chose to go down with his ship. He dismissed his crew, gave permission for the wounded to be saved, and remained on the bridge. He was 49 years old. After the war, Japanese naval officers identified Yamaguchi as the commander most likely to have changed the war's outcome had he survived Midway. Japan lost its best admiral and four carriers on the same day.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Admiral Yamaguchi Tamon on Hiryu's bridge as the carrier sinks, choosing to remain as his crew evacuates — the admiral Japan considered its best, lost with the last Japanese carrier at Midway. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Battle of Midway — The Numbers
scenes.narration: The Battle of Midway lasted four days: June 4-7, 1942. Japan lost four fleet carriers, one heavy cruiser, 248 aircraft, and approximately 3,000 men. America lost one carrier, one destroyer, 150 aircraft, and 307 men. The disparity in aircraft losses was almost exactly reversed from Pearl Harbor six months earlier. The disparity in carriers lost was decisive: Japan had six fleet carriers before Midway and two after. America had three before and two after — and was building more at a rate Japan could not match. The industrial arithmetic of carrier warfare decided the war within the battle.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Battle of Midway statistics and comparison — the numbers that show the strategic reversal, four Japanese carriers against one American, the arithmetic of industrial production underlying tactical outcomes. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: America's Carrier Production — The Industrial War
scenes.narration: In 1942 alone, the United States commissioned or laid down 17 fleet and escort carriers. Japan commissioned 5. By 1944, American shipyards were producing carrier tonnage faster than Japan could sink it. The Essex-class carriers — 27 commissioned between 1943 and 1945 — each carried 90 aircraft, compared to the 60-70 of the Japanese carriers they replaced. Japan's shipbuilding capacity, constrained by steel and fuel shortages, could not compete. Midway was a battle. The carrier production race was the war. America was winning it regardless of Midway. Midway simply made it irreversible sooner.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. American shipyards in 1942-1944 building Essex-class carriers — the industrial scale that Japan could not match, the assembly lines of war that would overwhelm Japanese production. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Pacific War's Cost — Three More Years
scenes.narration: Midway decided who would win. It did not end the killing. Three more years of war followed. Guadalcanal. The Marianas. The Philippines. Iwo Jima. Okinawa. The firebombing of Japan's cities. The atomic bombs at Hiroshima and Nagasaki. Approximately 3 million Japanese and 100,000 Americans died in the Pacific after Midway. The war whose outcome was settled in five minutes on June 4, 1942 killed millions more before it ended on September 2, 1945. This is the truth about decisive battles: they decide who wins. They do not decide when the losing side stops fighting.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Pacific war timeline after Midway — Guadalcanal, Iwo Jima, the firebombing of Tokyo, Hiroshima, the three more years of death that followed the battle that decided the outcome. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Nimitz vs Yamamoto — The Two Admirals
scenes.narration: The two admirals who faced each other at Midway represented opposite philosophies. Yamamoto planned the war-ending decisive battle, committing overwhelming force to force a single definitive engagement. Nimitz played a calculated hand: use intelligence to choose the ground, commit what he had, accept losses. Yamamoto lost because his intelligence was wrong and his plan was too complex. Nimitz won because his intelligence was right and his plan was simple. The battle that decided the Pacific war was, at its core, a contest between an admiral who knew what was coming and one who didn't.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Contrast of Yamamoto and Nimitz — the two admirals who shaped the Pacific war, the intelligence advantage that gave Nimitz the decisive edge at the battle that ended Japanese dominance. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Men at the Bottom of the Pacific
scenes.narration: At the bottom of the Pacific Ocean, approximately 1,000 feet down, lie the wrecks of four Japanese carriers and the memories of the men who sailed them. The aviators who had bombed Pearl Harbor, raided Ceylon, sunk British warships in the Indian Ocean — they died in burning water off a tiny atoll most Americans had never heard of. On the American side, the men of Torpedo Squadron 8 lie nearby. All of them died in the same ocean, on the same day, within sight of each other. A coral reef in the Central Pacific holds everything that the Pacific war was, and everything it decided.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. The Pacific Ocean floor — the resting place of the Midway carriers, the memorial quality of the vast deep water above them, the quiet permanence of history's most decisive naval battle. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: The Intelligence Edge — Why America Won Before the Battle
scenes.narration: Midway is studied in every military intelligence school in the world because it demonstrates a fundamental principle: knowing your enemy's intentions is worth more than numerical equality. America's three carriers were outnumbered by Japan's four. American aircraft were technically inferior. American pilots were less experienced. But Nimitz knew when Japan was coming, from which direction, in what strength, and with what objectives. That intelligence transformed a defensive situation into an ambush. The battle demonstrated something that still shapes modern warfare: the side with better intelligence defeats the side with more ships.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Intelligence analysis and battle outcome comparison — the intelligence chain from codebreaking to combat decision, demonstrating how information transformed American inferiority into strategic advantage. 16:9 cinematic.

scenes.title: Midway Today — A National Monument
scenes.narration: The Battle of Midway was designated a National Monument in 2000. The atoll remains largely unchanged from 1942 — Laysan albatrosses still nest there in vast numbers. The runways are deteriorating. The old Pan American seaplane base is a ruin. The navy facilities are abandoned. But every June 4, ceremonies are held on Midway and in Washington remembering the pilots who didn't come back, the torpedo bomber crews who died in a hopeless attack that made a decisive attack possible, and the codebreakers in a basement in Pearl Harbor who read enough of the enemy's mail to change history.
scenes.visual_prompt: Gods & Glory cinematic documentary. Midway Atoll today — the albatross colony on the unchanged landscape, the deteriorating runways and navy facilities, the June 4 commemorations honoring the men who fought there. 16:9 cinematic.
